title: Variables and print
description: Store data in variables and show messages on screen
intro: Welcome! Every program needs to remember things and tell us what's going on. Variables remember; print shows.
learningObjectives: Create variables to store strings and numbers
Combine variables and text in output
goalDescription: Print a friendly greeting using at least one variable.

content.introduction: Variables are labels for values. print() is how the program talks back.

steps.title: Print a message
steps.description: Use print() to display 'Hello, world!'
steps.initialCode: # Print a hello message
steps.solution: print('Hello, world!')
hints: The whole line is print('Hello, world!')

steps.title: Store a name in a variable
steps.description: Make a variable named name with your favorite name, then print it.
steps.initialCode: # Set name to a string, then print it
steps.solution:
name = 'Pixel'
print(name)
hints: Quotes around the name make it a string

steps.title: Combine words
steps.description: Print a greeting that uses your name variable in a sentence.
steps.initialCode:
name = 'Pixel'
# Print a sentence that includes name
steps.solution:
name = 'Pixel'
print('Hi,', name, 'nice to meet you!')
hints: print() can take multiple arguments separated by commas
It puts spaces between them automatically
Try print('Hi,', name, 'nice to meet you!')

title: Numbers and math
description: Do arithmetic and store the results
intro: Computers love math. Let's add, subtract, and store numbers.
learningObjectives: Print the result of a calculation
goalDescription: Compute and print the area of a rectangle.

content.introduction: Python is a calculator. Variables can hold numbers too.

steps.title: Add two numbers
steps.description: Make two number variables and print their sum.
steps.initialCode: # Create two number variables and print their sum
steps.solution:
a = 7
b = 3
print(a + b)
hints: Use + between two variables to add them

steps.title: Rectangle area
steps.description: Set width and height, then print the area.
steps.initialCode:
width = 5
height = 3
# Print the area (width times height)
steps.solution:
width = 5
height = 3
area = width * height
print(area)
hints: You can store the result in a third variable

title: Conditionals
description: Make decisions in code with if / elif / else
intro: Programs make choices. if checks a condition; else handles the other case.
goalDescription: Print whether a number is positive or negative.

content.introduction: if runs the code under it only when the condition is True. else covers the other case.

steps.title: Pick the bigger number
steps.description: Set a and b to numbers; print the bigger one. Use if/else.
steps.initialCode:
a = 8
b = 12
# Print the bigger one
hints: if a > b: starts a conditional
Indent the line under it with 4 spaces
Add else: for the other case

steps.title: Even or odd
steps.description: Set n to a number; print 'even' if n % 2 is 0, else 'odd'. Don't use eval to cheat.
steps.initialCode:
n = 14
# Use if/else to print 'even' or 'odd'

title: Loops
description: Repeat actions with for and range
intro: Loops are how programs do the same thing many times without copy-paste.
learningObjectives: Loop over a list of values

content.introduction: range(1, 6) gives 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. for loops over them, running the indented code each time.

steps.title: Count to five
steps.description: Print the numbers 1 through 5, one per line.
steps.initialCode: # Print 1 to 5 on separate lines
steps.solution:
for i in range(1, 6):
    print(i)
hints: stop is one past the last number you want

steps.title: Sum the first ten numbers
steps.description: Use a loop to compute 1 + 2 + ... + 10. Print the total. Keep your code flat — no loops inside loops.
steps.initialCode:
total = 0
# Loop 1..10 and add each to total, then print total
steps.solution:
total = 0
for i in range(1, 11):
    total = total + i
print(total)
hints: Inside the loop: total = total + i

title: Functions
description: Bundle reusable steps into named functions
intro: When you do the same thing twice, write a function. It takes inputs (parameters) and gives back a result (return).
goalDescription: Write a function that doubles a number.

content.introduction: def name(parameters): defines a function. return sends a value back to whoever called it.

steps.title: Greet by name
steps.description: Define greet(name) that returns 'Hello, ' + name. Call it with 'Pixel' and print the result.
steps.initialCode: # Define greet(name) and print greet('Pixel')
hints: After the function, call it: print(greet('Pixel'))

steps.title: Add two numbers
steps.description: Define add(a, b) that returns a + b. Print add(7, 5).
steps.initialCode: # Define add(a, b) and print add(7, 5)
hints: def add(a, b): with two parameters

title: First Pygame draw
description: Open a window, set a background, and draw a circle
intro: Time for graphics! Pygame is a library for games. We'll import it, set up a window, and draw a shape.
goalDescription: Open a Pygame window and draw a circle in the middle.
previewCode:
import pygame
pygame.init()
screen = pygame.display.set_mode((400, 300))
screen.fill((30, 30, 30))
pygame.draw.circle(screen, (255, 100, 200), (200, 150), 40)

content.introduction: Pygame's draw functions paint shapes onto a screen surface. We initialize the engine, create a screen, then draw.

steps.title: Import and initialize
steps.description: Import pygame, call pygame.init(), and create a screen surface that's 400 by 300.
steps.initialCode: # Import pygame, init it, and create a 400x300 screen
steps.solution:
import pygame
pygame.init()
screen = pygame.display.set_mode((400, 300))

steps.title: Draw a circle
steps.description: After setting up the screen, fill it with a dark color and draw a pink circle in the middle.
steps.initialCode:
import pygame
pygame.init()
screen = pygame.display.set_mode((400, 300))
# Fill the screen and draw a circle
steps.solution:
import pygame
pygame.init()
screen = pygame.display.set_mode((400, 300))
screen.fill((30, 30, 30))
pygame.draw.circle(screen, (255, 100, 200), (200, 150), 40)
hints: screen.fill((r, g, b)) paints the whole surface
(200, 150) is the middle of a 400x300 screen

title: Lists hold many things
description: Store and process collections with Python lists
intro: A list keeps multiple values in order. We'll build one, walk it with a loop, and use len() to count.
goalDescription: Make a list of three colors, print each one, then print the count.
previewCode:
colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue']
for c in colors:
    print(c)
print(len(colors))

content.introduction: Lists are written with square brackets. Items are separated by commas. You can loop through them with `for`, ask `len()` for the size, and index with `colors[0]`.

steps.title: Make a list
steps.description: Create a list called `colors` with three strings: 'red', 'green', 'blue'. Then print the list.
steps.initialCode: # Make a list of three colors and print it
steps.solution:
colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue']
print(colors)
hints: Use [ and ] to start and end the list

steps.title: Loop through the list
steps.description: Loop through `colors` with a `for` loop and print each color on its own line.
steps.initialCode:
colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue']
# Print each color
hints: for c in colors: walks each item
print(c) inside the loop prints one per line
Indent the body of the loop with 4 spaces

steps.title: Count with len()
steps.description: After the loop, print `len(colors)` so the user sees how many items were in the list.
steps.initialCode:
colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue']
for c in colors:
    print(c)
# Print the count
steps.solution:
colors = ['red', 'green', 'blue']
for c in colors:
    print(c)
print(len(colors))
hints: Wrap it in print() to show it

title: Save and read files
description: Use Python's open() to write and read text files
intro: Files let your program remember things between runs. We'll write a line, read it back, and print what we got.
goalDescription: Write 'hello, file' into note.txt, then read it back and print what's there.
previewCode:
with open('note.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('hello, file')
with open('note.txt', 'r') as f:
    print(f.read())

content.introduction: Pyodide gives you a virtual filesystem in the browser. open(name, 'w') writes; open(name, 'r') reads. Use a `with` block so the file is closed automatically when you're done.

steps.title: Write to a file
steps.description: Open `note.txt` for writing and write the text `hello, file` into it.
steps.initialCode: # Write 'hello, file' into note.txt
steps.solution:
with open('note.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('hello, file')
hints: with open(...) as f: gives you a file object named f

steps.title: Read it back
steps.description: After writing, open `note.txt` for reading and print what you read.
steps.initialCode:
with open('note.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('hello, file')
# Read it back and print it
steps.solution:
with open('note.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('hello, file')
with open('note.txt', 'r') as f:
    print(f.read())
hints: f.read() returns the whole file as a string
Wrap it in print() to show it

title: Classes group data and behavior
description: Create your first class with __init__ and a method
intro: Classes let you bundle data (attributes) and behavior (methods) into one thing. We'll define a Player class with a name and a greet() method.
goalDescription: Define a Player class with a name attribute and a greet() method, then create one and call greet().

content.introduction: A class is a recipe for objects. `__init__` runs when you create one (Player('Pixel')) and stores attributes on `self`. Methods are functions that take `self` as their first argument.

steps.title: Define the class
steps.description: Define a class called `Player` with an `__init__(self, name)` that stores `self.name = name`.
steps.initialCode: # Define class Player with __init__ that stores self.name

steps.title: Add a method
steps.description: Add a `greet(self)` method that prints `Hi, I am {self.name}`.
hints: Use print(f'...') to format the string
Indent the method inside the class with 4 spaces

steps.title: Use the class
steps.description: Create a Player named 'Pixel' and call its `greet()` method.
hints: Save it in a variable: p = Player('Pixel')
